essential_questions: How do elements combine to form compounds?
Why are there more compounds than elements?
learning_targets: I can explain that a finite number of elements combine to form many compounds.
I can describe how elements and compounds make up all matter.
sample_assessment: There are about 118 known elements. How is it possible that millions of different substances exist? A) New elements are created every day B) Elements combine in many different ways to form compounds C) Most substances are made of only one element D) Substances are not made of elements

benchmark: Recognize that elements are grouped in the periodic table according to similarities of their properties.
item_specs: Students must recognize that the periodic table organizes elements by similar properties.
essential_questions: How are elements organized in the periodic table?
What do elements in the same group have in common?
learning_targets: I can explain how elements are organized in the periodic table.
I can identify elements with similar properties based on their position in the periodic table.
sample_assessment: Elements in the same group (column) of the periodic table have: A) The same number of protons B) Similar chemical properties C) The same mass D) The same color

benchmark: Explore the scientific theory of atoms (also known as atomic theory) by recognizing that atoms are the smallest unit of an element and are composed of sub-atomic particles (electrons surrounding a nucleus containing protons and neutrons).
item_specs: Students must describe atomic structure including protons, neutrons, and electrons.
essential_questions: What are atoms made of?
What are the sub-atomic particles and where are they located?
learning_targets: I can describe the structure of an atom including protons, neutrons, and electrons.
I can explain that atoms are the smallest unit of an element.
sample_assessment: Which sub-atomic particle is found orbiting the nucleus of an atom? A) Proton B) Neutron C) Electron D) Nucleus

benchmark: Identify basic examples of and compare and classify the properties of compounds, including acids, bases, and salts.
item_specs: Students must identify, compare, and classify compounds including acids, bases, and salts.
essential_questions: What are the properties of acids, bases, and salts?
How can you classify a substance as an acid, base, or salt?
learning_targets: I can identify properties of acids, bases, and salts.
I can compare and classify compounds as acids, bases, or salts.
sample_assessment: A substance with a pH of 3 would be classified as: A) A base B) Neutral C) An acid D) A salt

benchmark: Distinguish among mixtures (including solutions) and pure substances.
item_specs: Students must distinguish between mixtures (including solutions) and pure substances.
essential_questions: What is the difference between a mixture and a pure substance?
What are the characteristics of a solution?
learning_targets: I can distinguish between mixtures and pure substances.
I can identify solutions as a type of mixture.
sample_assessment: Salt water is an example of: A) A pure substance B) An element C) A heterogeneous mixture D) A homogeneous mixture (solution)

benchmark: Explore the Law of Conservation of Mass by demonstrating and concluding that mass is conserved when substances undergo physical and chemical changes.
item_specs: Students must demonstrate and explain the Law of Conservation of Mass during physical and chemical changes.
essential_questions: What is the Law of Conservation of Mass?
How is mass conserved during physical and chemical changes?
learning_targets: I can explain the Law of Conservation of Mass.
I can demonstrate that mass is conserved during physical and chemical changes.
sample_assessment: In a sealed container, 10 grams of substance A reacts with 5 grams of substance B. What is the total mass of the products? A) 5 grams B) 10 grams C) 15 grams D) 20 grams

benchmark: Differentiate between physical changes and chemical changes.
item_specs: Students must differentiate between physical changes (changes in form) and chemical changes (changes that produce new substances).
essential_questions: What is the difference between a physical change and a chemical change?
What are indicators of a chemical change?
learning_targets: I can differentiate between physical and chemical changes.
I can identify indicators of a chemical change.
sample_assessment: Which is an example of a chemical change? A) Cutting paper B) Melting ice C) Burning wood D) Dissolving sugar in water

benchmark: Investigate and describe how temperature influences chemical changes.
item_specs: Students must describe how temperature affects the rate and occurrence of chemical changes.
essential_questions: How does temperature affect chemical changes?
Why do chemical reactions happen faster at higher temperatures?
learning_targets: I can describe how temperature influences chemical changes.
I can explain why increasing temperature generally speeds up chemical reactions.
sample_assessment: Food spoils faster when left out at room temperature compared to in the refrigerator. This is because: A) Cold temperatures speed up chemical reactions B) Higher temperatures slow down chemical reactions C) Higher temperatures speed up the chemical reactions that cause spoiling D) Temperature has no effect on chemical changes

benchmark: Cite evidence used to develop and verify the scientific theory of the Big Bang (also known as the Big Bang Theory) of the origin of the universe.
item_specs: Students must cite evidence supporting the Big Bang Theory.
essential_questions: What evidence supports the Big Bang Theory?
How did the universe begin?
learning_targets: I can describe evidence supporting the Big Bang Theory.
I can explain how cosmic background radiation supports the Big Bang Theory.
sample_assessment: Which evidence supports the Big Bang Theory? A) The Moon orbits Earth B) Stars emit light C) Galaxies are moving away from each other (redshift) D) Seasons change on Earth

benchmark: Identify patterns in the organization and distribution of matter in the universe and the forces that determine them.
item_specs: Students must identify patterns in how matter is organized in the universe.
essential_questions: How is matter organized in the universe?
What forces determine the distribution of matter?
learning_targets: I can identify patterns in the organization of matter from atoms to galaxies.
I can explain how gravity influences the distribution of matter.
sample_assessment: What force is primarily responsible for the large-scale structure of the universe? A) Electromagnetic force B) Nuclear force C) Gravity D) Friction

benchmark: Describe and predict how the initial mass of a star determines its evolution.
item_specs: Students must describe how a star's mass determines its life cycle and fate.
essential_questions: How does a star's mass affect its life cycle?
What are the possible fates of a star?
learning_targets: I can describe the stages of stellar evolution.
I can predict a star's fate based on its initial mass.
sample_assessment: A star with a very large mass will most likely end as: A) A white dwarf B) A black hole C) A red dwarf D) A planet

benchmark: Explain the physical properties of the Sun and its dynamic nature and connect them to conditions and events on Earth.
item_specs: Students must describe the Sun's properties and how solar activity affects Earth.
essential_questions: What are the physical properties of the Sun?
How does solar activity affect Earth?
learning_targets: I can describe the physical properties of the Sun.
I can explain how solar events like solar flares affect Earth.
sample_assessment: Solar flares can affect Earth by: A) Causing earthquakes B) Disrupting radio communications and power grids C) Changing the tilt of Earth's axis D) Creating new continents

benchmark: Explain the formation of planetary systems based on our knowledge of our Solar System and apply this knowledge to newly discovered planetary systems.
item_specs: Students must explain how planetary systems form from nebulae.
essential_questions: How did our solar system form?
How do we apply this knowledge to other planetary systems?
learning_targets: I can explain the nebular hypothesis of solar system formation.
I can compare our solar system to newly discovered planetary systems.
sample_assessment: According to the nebular hypothesis, the solar system formed from: A) A collision of two stars B) A rotating cloud of gas and dust C) The explosion of a planet D) Material from another galaxy

benchmark: Develop logical connections through physical principles, including Kepler's and Newton's Laws about the relationships and the effects of Earth, Moon, and Sun on each other.
item_specs: Students must apply Kepler's and Newton's laws to explain Earth-Moon-Sun interactions.
essential_questions: How do Kepler's laws describe planetary motion?
How does gravity govern the Earth-Moon-Sun system?
learning_targets: I can apply Kepler's laws to describe orbital motion.
I can explain how Newton's law of gravitation governs the Earth-Moon-Sun system.
sample_assessment: According to Kepler's first law, planets orbit the Sun in: A) Perfect circles B) Ellipses C) Straight lines D) Spirals

benchmark: Connect the concepts of radiation and the electromagnetic spectrum to the use of historical and newly-developed observational tools.
item_specs: Students must connect electromagnetic radiation to astronomical observation tools.
essential_questions: How do scientists use the electromagnetic spectrum to study the universe?
What tools are used to observe different types of radiation?
learning_targets: I can describe how different types of electromagnetic radiation are used in astronomy.
I can explain how telescopes and other tools detect different wavelengths.
sample_assessment: A radio telescope detects: A) Visible light from stars B) Radio waves from space C) Sound waves from planets D) Gravity waves only

benchmark: Analyze the broad effects of space exploration on the economy and culture of Florida.
item_specs: Students must analyze how space exploration has affected Florida economically and culturally.
essential_questions: How has space exploration affected Florida's economy?
What cultural impacts has the space program had on Florida?
learning_targets: I can analyze the economic impact of space exploration on Florida.
I can describe the cultural effects of the space program.
sample_assessment: How has the space program primarily affected Florida's economy? A) Decreased tourism B) Created aerospace jobs and attracted technology industries C) Reduced population D) Eliminated agriculture

benchmark: Distinguish the various methods of measuring astronomical distances and apply each in appropriate situations.
item_specs: Students must describe methods for measuring distances in space.
essential_questions: How do astronomers measure distances in space?
What is the difference between a light-year and a parsec?
learning_targets: I can distinguish between methods of measuring astronomical distances.
I can apply appropriate measurement methods for different distances.
sample_assessment: Which method is used to measure the distance to nearby stars? A) Radar B) Stellar parallax C) Redshift D) Standard candle

benchmark: Describe and differentiate the layers of Earth and the interactions among them.
item_specs: Students must describe Earth's layers and how they interact.
essential_questions: What are the layers of Earth?
How do Earth's layers interact with each other?
learning_targets: I can describe the composition and properties of each layer of Earth.
I can explain how Earth's layers interact.
sample_assessment: Which layer of Earth is liquid and composed mostly of iron and nickel? A) Inner core B) Outer core C) Mantle D) Crust

benchmark: Connect surface features to surface processes that are responsible for their formation.
item_specs: Students must connect landforms to the processes that created them.
essential_questions: How do surface processes shape landforms?
What processes are responsible for mountain formation?
learning_targets: I can connect surface features to the processes that formed them.
I can explain how weathering, erosion, and deposition shape the landscape.
sample_assessment: The Grand Canyon was primarily formed by: A) Volcanic eruption B) Earthquake activity C) Water erosion over millions of years D) Glacial movement

benchmark: Analyze the scientific theory of plate tectonics and identify related major processes and features as a result of moving plates.
item_specs: Students must analyze plate tectonics and identify geological features formed at plate boundaries.
essential_questions: What is the theory of plate tectonics?
What geological features result from plate movement?
learning_targets: I can describe the three types of plate boundaries.
I can identify geological features formed at each type of boundary.
sample_assessment: Deep ocean trenches form at: A) Divergent boundaries B) Convergent boundaries where one plate subducts C) Transform boundaries D) Hot spots

benchmark: Analyze how specific geologic processes and features are expressed in Florida and elsewhere.
item_specs: Students must analyze geological processes specific to Florida.
essential_questions: What geological processes shape Florida?
Why is Florida prone to sinkholes?
learning_targets: I can describe geological features found in Florida.
I can explain how karst topography forms in Florida.
sample_assessment: Sinkholes in Florida are primarily caused by: A) Earthquakes B) Volcanic activity C) Dissolution of limestone bedrock D) Glacial retreat

benchmark: Describe the geologic development of the present day oceans and identify commonly found features.
item_specs: Students must describe how oceans formed and identify seafloor features.
essential_questions: How did the present-day oceans form?
What features are found on the ocean floor?
learning_targets: I can describe the geological development of the oceans.
I can identify features of the ocean floor.
sample_assessment: Which feature on the ocean floor is created by seafloor spreading? A) Ocean trench B) Mid-ocean ridge C) Continental shelf D) Abyssal plain

benchmark: Analyze past, present, and potential future consequences to the environment resulting from various energy production technologies.
item_specs: Students must analyze environmental consequences of energy production technologies.
essential_questions: How do different energy sources impact the environment?
What are the trade-offs of various energy production methods?
learning_targets: I can analyze environmental impacts of fossil fuels, nuclear, and renewable energy.
I can evaluate the sustainability of different energy production methods.
sample_assessment: Which energy source produces the least greenhouse gas emissions during operation? A) Coal B) Natural gas C) Solar D) Oil

benchmark: Analyze the movement of matter and energy through the different biogeochemical cycles, including water and carbon.
item_specs: Students must analyze how matter and energy move through biogeochemical cycles.
essential_questions: How does matter cycle through ecosystems?
What drives the water and carbon cycles?
learning_targets: I can describe the water cycle and carbon cycle.
I can analyze how energy drives biogeochemical cycles.
sample_assessment: Which process moves carbon from the atmosphere into living organisms? A) Combustion B) Photosynthesis C) Respiration D) Evaporation

benchmark: Analyze the causes of the various kinds of surface and deep water motion within the oceans and their impacts on the transfer of energy between the poles and the equator.
item_specs: Students must analyze causes of ocean water motion and its role in energy transfer.
essential_questions: What causes ocean currents?
How do ocean currents transfer energy between the poles and the equator?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes of surface and deep water currents.
I can explain how ocean currents redistribute heat energy.
sample_assessment: Deep ocean currents are primarily driven by: A) Wind B) Differences in water density due to temperature and salinity C) Tidal forces D) Earthquakes

benchmark: Differentiate and describe the various interactions among Earth systems, including: atmosphere, hydrosphere, cryosphere, geosphere, and biosphere.
item_specs: Students must differentiate among Earth's systems and describe their interactions.
essential_questions: What are Earth's major systems?
How do Earth's systems interact with each other?
learning_targets: I can identify and describe Earth's five major systems.
I can explain how interactions among Earth's systems affect the planet.
sample_assessment: A volcanic eruption releasing ash into the atmosphere is an interaction between which two Earth systems? A) Hydrosphere and biosphere B) Geosphere and atmosphere C) Cryosphere and biosphere D) Atmosphere and hydrosphere

benchmark: Summarize the conditions that contribute to the climate of a geographic area, including the relationships to lakes and oceans.
item_specs: Students must summarize conditions that determine a region's climate.
essential_questions: What factors determine a region's climate?
How do bodies of water influence nearby climates?
learning_targets: I can summarize the factors that contribute to a region's climate.
I can explain how proximity to water bodies affects climate.
sample_assessment: A coastal city tends to have milder temperatures than an inland city at the same latitude because: A) It receives more sunlight B) Water moderates temperature changes C) It is at a higher altitude D) Wind patterns are different

benchmark: Predict future weather conditions based on present observations and conceptual models and recognize limitations and uncertainties of such predictions.
item_specs: Students must use weather data to make predictions and recognize limitations.
essential_questions: How do meteorologists predict weather?
Why are weather predictions sometimes inaccurate?
learning_targets: I can predict weather conditions based on observations and models.
I can recognize limitations of weather predictions.
sample_assessment: Why do long-range weather forecasts become less accurate over time? A) The instruments break down B) Weather systems are complex and chaotic C) Scientists stop collecting data D) Satellites stop working

benchmark: Relate the formation of severe weather to the various physical factors.
item_specs: Students must explain how physical factors contribute to severe weather formation.
essential_questions: What conditions lead to severe weather?
How do hurricanes and tornadoes form?
learning_targets: I can explain the physical factors that lead to severe weather.
I can describe how hurricanes, tornadoes, and thunderstorms form.
sample_assessment: Hurricanes form over warm ocean water because: A) Cold water provides energy B) Warm water evaporates and provides energy through condensation C) The ocean floor is unstable D) Gravity is weaker over water

benchmark: Identify, analyze, and relate the internal (Earth system) and external (astronomical) conditions that contribute to global climate change.
item_specs: Students must analyze internal and external factors contributing to climate change.
essential_questions: What internal and external factors contribute to climate change?
How do greenhouse gases affect global temperature?
learning_targets: I can identify factors that contribute to global climate change.
I can analyze the relationship between greenhouse gases and global warming.
sample_assessment: Which is an external (astronomical) factor that contributes to climate change? A) Volcanic eruptions B) Deforestation C) Changes in Earth's orbital pattern (Milankovitch cycles) D) Burning fossil fuels

benchmark: Explain how various atmospheric, oceanic, and hydrologic conditions in Florida have influenced and can influence human behavior, both individually and collectively.
item_specs: Students must explain how Florida's environmental conditions influence human behavior.
essential_questions: How do Florida's weather and climate conditions affect residents?
How do natural hazards in Florida influence human decisions?
learning_targets: I can explain how Florida's climate conditions influence human behavior.
I can describe how hurricanes and other hazards affect communities.
sample_assessment: Florida building codes require hurricane-resistant construction because: A) It is cheaper to build B) The state frequently experiences hurricanes C) Earthquakes are common D) Snow loads are heavy

benchmark: Cite evidence that the ocean has had a significant influence on climate change by absorbing, storing, and moving heat, carbon, and water.
item_specs: Students must cite evidence of the ocean's influence on climate.
essential_questions: How does the ocean influence climate?
How does the ocean absorb and store carbon?
learning_targets: I can cite evidence that the ocean influences climate change.
I can explain how the ocean absorbs, stores, and moves heat and carbon.
sample_assessment: The ocean influences climate change by: A) Releasing all heat immediately B) Absorbing and storing large amounts of carbon dioxide and heat C) Reflecting all sunlight D) Producing greenhouse gases only

benchmark: Describe the scientific theory of cells (cell theory) and relate the history of its discovery to the process of science.
item_specs: Students must describe cell theory and how it was developed through scientific investigation.
essential_questions: What are the three parts of cell theory?
How did the development of the microscope contribute to cell theory?
learning_targets: I can describe the three principles of cell theory.
I can explain how scientists contributed to the development of cell theory.
sample_assessment: Which is a principle of cell theory? A) All cells contain a nucleus B) All living things are made of cells C) Cells can arise spontaneously D) All cells are the same size

benchmark: Relate structure to function for the components of plant and animal cells. Explain the role of cell membranes as a highly selective barrier (passive and active transport).
item_specs: Students must relate the structure of cell components to their function and explain membrane transport.
essential_questions: How does the structure of a cell organelle relate to its function?
How does the cell membrane regulate what enters and exits the cell?
learning_targets: I can relate the structure of organelles to their functions.
I can explain passive and active transport across the cell membrane.
sample_assessment: Which process requires energy to move molecules across the cell membrane? A) Osmosis B) Diffusion C) Active transport D) Facilitated diffusion

benchmark: Compare and contrast the general structures of plant and animal cells. Compare and contrast the general structures of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
item_specs: Students must compare and contrast different cell types based on their structures.
essential_questions: What structures are unique to plant cells?
How do prokaryotic cells differ from eukaryotic cells?
learning_targets: I can compare and contrast plant and animal cells.
I can compare and contrast prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
sample_assessment: Which structure is found in plant cells but NOT in animal cells? A) Mitochondria B) Cell membrane C) Chloroplast D) Ribosome

benchmark: Compare and contrast structure and function of various types of microscopes.
item_specs: Students must compare different types of microscopes and their uses.
essential_questions: How do different microscopes work?
When would you use one type of microscope over another?
learning_targets: I can compare light microscopes and electron microscopes.
I can explain the advantages and limitations of different microscopes.
sample_assessment: Which type of microscope would be best to view the surface of a virus? A) Light microscope B) Dissecting microscope C) Scanning electron microscope D) Hand lens

benchmark: Explain the significance of genetic factors, environmental factors, and pathogenic agents to health from the perspectives of both individual and public health.
item_specs: Students must explain how genetic, environmental, and pathogenic factors affect health.
essential_questions: How do genetic and environmental factors influence health?
What role do pathogens play in public health?
learning_targets: I can explain how genetic factors affect individual health.
I can describe how environmental factors and pathogens impact public health.
sample_assessment: Sickle cell disease is caused by: A) A virus B) Environmental pollution C) A genetic mutation D) Poor nutrition

benchmark: Relate the structure of each of the major plant organs and tissues to physiological processes.
item_specs: Students must relate plant organ structure to function.
essential_questions: How does the structure of plant organs relate to their function?
What are the major plant tissues and their roles?
learning_targets: I can describe the structure and function of major plant organs.
I can explain how plant tissues carry out physiological processes.
sample_assessment: Which plant tissue is responsible for transporting water from the roots to the leaves? A) Phloem B) Xylem C) Epidermis D) Pith

benchmark: Identify the major parts of the brain on diagrams or models.
item_specs: Students must identify the major structures of the brain and their functions.
essential_questions: What are the major parts of the brain?
What functions are controlled by each part of the brain?
learning_targets: I can identify the cerebrum, cerebellum, and brain stem on a diagram.
I can describe the function of each major brain region.
sample_assessment: Which part of the brain controls balance and coordination? A) Cerebrum B) Cerebellum C) Medulla oblongata D) Frontal lobe

benchmark: Describe the factors affecting blood flow through the cardiovascular system.
item_specs: Students must describe factors that affect blood flow including blood pressure and vessel diameter.
essential_questions: What factors affect blood flow?
How does the cardiovascular system maintain blood flow?
learning_targets: I can describe factors that affect blood pressure and blood flow.
I can explain how the heart and blood vessels work together.
sample_assessment: Which factor would increase blood pressure? A) Vasodilation B) Decreased heart rate C) Vasoconstriction D) Decreased blood volume

benchmark: Explain the basic functions of the human immune system, including specific and nonspecific immune response, vaccines, and antibiotics.
item_specs: Students must explain the immune system's specific and nonspecific responses.
essential_questions: How does the immune system protect the body?
What is the difference between specific and nonspecific immune responses?
learning_targets: I can explain the difference between specific and nonspecific immunity.
I can describe how vaccines and antibiotics help fight disease.
sample_assessment: Which is an example of a nonspecific immune response? A) Antibody production B) Skin acting as a barrier C) T cell activation D) Vaccination

benchmark: Explain how the scientific theory of evolution is supported by the fossil record, comparative anatomy, comparative embryology, biogeography, molecular biology, and observed evolutionary change.
item_specs: Students must explain the multiple lines of evidence supporting the theory of evolution.
essential_questions: What evidence supports the theory of evolution?
How do fossils provide evidence of evolution?
learning_targets: I can identify multiple lines of evidence supporting evolution.
I can explain how comparative anatomy and molecular biology support evolution.
sample_assessment: Homologous structures in different species suggest that these species: A) Live in the same habitat B) Share a common ancestor C) Eat the same food D) Evolved independently

benchmark: Discuss the use of molecular clocks to estimate how long ago various groups of organisms diverged evolutionarily from one another.
item_specs: Students must explain how molecular clocks estimate evolutionary divergence times.
essential_questions: What is a molecular clock?
How do scientists use DNA to estimate when species diverged?
learning_targets: I can explain how molecular clocks work.
I can describe how DNA comparisons estimate evolutionary relationships.
sample_assessment: Two species have very similar DNA sequences. What does this suggest? A) They diverged recently B) They diverged long ago C) They are not related D) They live in the same environment

benchmark: Describe how biological diversity is increased by the origin of new species and how it is decreased by the natural process of extinction.
item_specs: Students must describe the processes that increase and decrease biodiversity.
essential_questions: How does speciation increase biodiversity?
What causes species to go extinct?
learning_targets: I can describe how new species originate.
I can explain how extinction decreases biodiversity.
sample_assessment: Geographic isolation can lead to speciation because: A) Organisms cannot find food B) Separated populations may evolve differently C) All organisms are the same D) Climate does not change

benchmark: Describe how and why organisms are hierarchically classified and based on evolutionary relationships.
item_specs: Students must describe the hierarchical classification system and how it reflects evolutionary relationships.
essential_questions: How are organisms classified?
Why is classification based on evolutionary relationships?
learning_targets: I can describe the levels of biological classification.
I can explain how classification reflects evolutionary relationships.
sample_assessment: Which level of classification contains the most organisms? A) Species B) Genus C) Family D) Kingdom

benchmark: Explain the reasons for changes in how organisms are classified.
item_specs: Students must explain why classification systems change over time.
essential_questions: Why has the classification of organisms changed over time?
How has molecular evidence changed classification?
learning_targets: I can explain why classification systems are revised.
I can describe how new evidence leads to reclassification.
sample_assessment: Why have some organisms been reclassified in recent years? A) Scientists were bored B) New molecular evidence revealed different evolutionary relationships C) The organisms changed species D) Old classification was random

benchmark: Discuss distinguishing characteristics of the domains and kingdoms of living organisms.
item_specs: Students must describe the characteristics that distinguish each domain and kingdom.
essential_questions: What are the three domains of life?
What characteristics distinguish each kingdom?
learning_targets: I can describe the three domains: Archaea, Bacteria, and Eukarya.
I can identify the characteristics of each kingdom.
sample_assessment: Which domain includes organisms that live in extreme environments such as hot springs? A) Bacteria B) Eukarya C) Archaea D) Protista

benchmark: Describe the scientific explanations of the origin of life on Earth.
item_specs: Students must describe scientific hypotheses about how life originated on Earth.
essential_questions: What are the scientific explanations for the origin of life?
What conditions on early Earth may have led to the first life forms?
learning_targets: I can describe the conditions on early Earth.
I can explain scientific hypotheses about the origin of life.
sample_assessment: The Miller-Urey experiment demonstrated that: A) Life can be created in a lab B) Organic molecules can form from inorganic molecules under early Earth conditions C) DNA was the first molecule of life D) Cells can form spontaneously

benchmark: Identify basic trends in hominid evolution from early ancestors six million years ago to modern humans, including brain size, jaw size, language, and manufacture of tools.
item_specs: Students must identify trends in human evolution over the last six million years.
essential_questions: What are the major trends in hominid evolution?
How have humans changed physically over time?
learning_targets: I can identify trends in brain size and jaw size in hominid evolution.
I can describe the development of language and tool use in human ancestors.
sample_assessment: Which trend is observed in hominid evolution? A) Decreasing brain size B) Increasing jaw size C) Increasing brain size D) Loss of bipedalism

benchmark: Describe the conditions required for natural selection, including: overproduction of offspring, inherited variation, and the struggle to survive, which result in differential reproductive success.
item_specs: Students must describe the conditions necessary for natural selection to occur.
essential_questions: What conditions are necessary for natural selection?
How does natural selection lead to adaptation?
learning_targets: I can describe the four conditions required for natural selection.
I can explain how differential reproductive success leads to evolution.
sample_assessment: Which is NOT a condition required for natural selection? A) Overproduction of offspring B) Inherited variation C) Struggle to survive D) Identical traits in all individuals

benchmark: Discuss mechanisms of evolutionary change other than natural selection such as genetic drift and gene flow.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to explain and compare different mechanisms of evolutionary change, including genetic drift and gene flow, using scenarios or examples. Items may include multiple-choice questions that assess understanding of these concepts in relation to natural selection.
essential_questions: What are the different mechanisms of evolutionary change?
How do genetic drift and gene flow contribute to evolution?
In what ways do these mechanisms differ from natural selection?
learning_targets: I can explain genetic drift and its effects on populations.
I can describe gene flow and its role in evolution.
I can compare mechanisms of evolutionary change.
sample_assessment: Which of the following best describes genetic drift? A) A change in allele frequencies due to random sampling events.

benchmark: Describe how mutation and genetic recombination increase genetic variation.
item_specs: Students must explain how mutations and recombination contribute to genetic diversity.
essential_questions: How do mutations increase genetic variation?
What is the role of genetic recombination in evolution?
learning_targets: I can describe how mutations create new alleles.
I can explain how genetic recombination during meiosis increases variation.
sample_assessment: How does crossing over during meiosis contribute to evolution? A) It decreases variation B) It increases genetic variation in offspring C) It prevents mutations D) It copies DNA exactly

benchmark: Use Mendel's laws of segregation and independent assortment to analyze patterns of inheritance.
item_specs: Students must apply Mendel's laws to predict inheritance patterns.
essential_questions: What are Mendel's laws of inheritance?
How can you predict the outcome of a genetic cross?
learning_targets: I can apply the law of segregation to predict offspring genotypes.
I can use Punnett squares to determine the probability of trait inheritance.
sample_assessment: If two heterozygous parents (Bb) are crossed, what percentage of offspring are expected to be homozygous recessive? A) 0% B) 25% C) 50% D) 75%

benchmark: Discuss observed inheritance patterns caused by various modes of inheritance, including dominant, recessive, codominant, sex-linked, polygenic, and multiple alleles.
item_specs: Students must explain various modes of inheritance beyond simple dominance.
essential_questions: What inheritance patterns exist beyond simple dominant/recessive?
How do codominance and incomplete dominance differ?
learning_targets: I can describe codominant, sex-linked, and polygenic inheritance.
I can predict outcomes of non-Mendelian inheritance patterns.
sample_assessment: A trait controlled by many genes that shows a wide range of phenotypes is called: A) Codominant B) Sex-linked C) Polygenic D) Recessive

benchmark: Describe the basic process of DNA replication and how it relates to the transmission and conservation of the genetic information.
item_specs: Students must describe DNA replication and its role in transmitting genetic information.
essential_questions: How is DNA replicated?
Why is accurate DNA replication important?
learning_targets: I can describe the steps of DNA replication.
I can explain how DNA replication conserves genetic information.
sample_assessment: During DNA replication, which enzyme unzips the double helix? A) DNA polymerase B) RNA polymerase C) Helicase D) Ligase

benchmark: Explain how mutations in the DNA sequence may or may not result in phenotypic change. Explain how mutations in gametes may result in phenotypic changes in offspring.
item_specs: Students must explain the effects of different types of mutations on phenotype.
essential_questions: How can mutations affect an organism's phenotype?
Why do some mutations have no effect?
learning_targets: I can explain how mutations may or may not change phenotype.
I can describe the difference between somatic and gametic mutations.
sample_assessment: A silent mutation does not change the phenotype because: A) It is repaired immediately B) It codes for the same amino acid C) It only occurs in gametes D) It deletes the gene

benchmark: Explain the basic processes of transcription and translation, and how they result in the expression of genes.
item_specs: Students must explain how genes are expressed through transcription and translation.
essential_questions: How does a gene become a protein?
What are the roles of mRNA, tRNA, and rRNA?
learning_targets: I can describe the process of transcription.
I can describe the process of translation.
I can explain how these processes result in gene expression.
sample_assessment: During transcription, which molecule is produced? A) DNA B) mRNA C) Protein D) tRNA

benchmark: Discuss the mechanisms for regulation of gene expression in prokaryotes and eukaryotes at transcription and translation level.
item_specs: Students must describe how gene expression is regulated in different organisms.
essential_questions: How is gene expression regulated?
Why don't all cells express the same genes?
learning_targets: I can describe gene regulation in prokaryotes using the operon model.
I can explain how gene expression is regulated in eukaryotes.
sample_assessment: In the lac operon, the repressor protein: A) Always blocks transcription B) Blocks transcription only when lactose is absent C) Activates transcription D) Destroys the mRNA

benchmark: Describe how viruses and bacteria transfer genetic material between cells and the role of this process in biotechnology.
item_specs: Students must describe mechanisms of horizontal gene transfer and their biotechnology applications.
essential_questions: How do viruses transfer genetic material?
How is gene transfer used in biotechnology?
learning_targets: I can describe how viruses and bacteria transfer genetic material.
I can explain how gene transfer is applied in biotechnology.
sample_assessment: When a bacteriophage transfers DNA from one bacterium to another, this is called: A) Conjugation B) Transduction C) Transformation D) Translation

benchmark: Explain the relationship between mutation, cell cycle, and uncontrolled cell growth potentially resulting in cancer.
item_specs: Students must explain how mutations in cell cycle genes can lead to cancer.
essential_questions: How are mutations related to cancer?
What happens when the cell cycle is not regulated?
learning_targets: I can explain how mutations in proto-oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes lead to cancer.
I can describe the relationship between cell cycle regulation and cancer.
sample_assessment: Cancer results from: A) Normal cell division B) Uncontrolled cell growth due to mutations C) Cells dividing too slowly D) Cells that stop growing

benchmark: Explain how and why the genetic code is universal and is common to almost all organisms.
item_specs: Students must explain why the genetic code is universal across organisms.
essential_questions: Why is the genetic code considered universal?
What does the universality of the genetic code suggest about life on Earth?
learning_targets: I can explain that the same codons code for the same amino acids in nearly all organisms.
I can describe how the universal genetic code supports common ancestry.
sample_assessment: The fact that the codon AUG codes for methionine in both bacteria and humans supports: A) Spontaneous generation B) Common ancestry of all life C) Independent evolution D) Random chance

benchmark: Evaluate the impact of biotechnology on the individual, society and the environment, including medical and ethical issues.
item_specs: Students must evaluate the benefits and risks of biotechnology applications.
essential_questions: What are the benefits and risks of biotechnology?
What ethical issues arise from genetic engineering?
learning_targets: I can evaluate the impact of biotechnology on individuals and society.
I can discuss ethical issues related to genetic engineering.
sample_assessment: Which is an ethical concern related to genetic engineering? A) Growing larger crops B) The potential for designer babies C) Making insulin for diabetics D) Using bacteria to clean oil spills

benchmark: Describe the basic anatomy and physiology of the human reproductive system. Describe the process of human development from fertilization to birth and major changes that occur in each trimester of pregnancy.
item_specs: Students must describe the human reproductive system and stages of development.
essential_questions: What are the major structures of the human reproductive system?
What changes occur during each trimester of pregnancy?
learning_targets: I can describe the anatomy of the human reproductive system.
I can describe the stages of human development from fertilization to birth.
sample_assessment: During which trimester does the embryo develop all major organ systems? A) First trimester B) Second trimester C) Third trimester D) After birth

benchmark: Describe the cell cycle, including the process of mitosis. Explain the role of mitosis in the formation of new cells and its importance in maintaining chromosome number during asexual reproduction. Introduction to cell division (B-H.1) The cell cycle (B-H.2) Describe phases of mitosis and the cell cycle (B-H.3) Identify phases of mitosis and the cell cycle in plant cells (B-H.4)
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to describe the phases of the cell cycle and mitosis, including their significance in asexual reproduction. Items may include diagrams for students to label or scenarios that require explanation of the importance of maintaining chromosome number.
essential_questions: What are the phases of the cell cycle and mitosis?
Why is mitosis important for asexual reproduction?
How does mitosis ensure the maintenance of chromosome number?
learning_targets: I can describe the phases of the cell cycle.
I can explain the process of mitosis.
I can identify the importance of mitosis in cell division.
sample_assessment: During which phase of mitosis do chromosomes align at the cell's equator? A) Prophase B) Metaphase C) Anaphase D) Telophase

benchmark: Describe the process of meiosis, including independent assortment and crossing over. Explain how reduction division results in the formation of haploid gametes or spores. Sexual reproduction and genetic variation: part I (B-K.1) Sexual reproduction and genetic variation: part II (B-K.)
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to describe the process of meiosis, including independent assortment and crossing over, and explain how these processes contribute to genetic variation. Items may involve scenarios or diagrams illustrating meiosis.
essential_questions: What are the stages of meiosis and their significance?
How does crossing over contribute to genetic diversity?
What is the outcome of meiosis in terms of gamete formation?
learning_targets: I can describe the process of meiosis.
I can explain independent assortment and crossing over.
I can identify the significance of meiosis in sexual reproduction.
sample_assessment: What is the main purpose of meiosis? A) To produce diploid cells B) To produce haploid gametes C) To replicate DNA D) To facilitate asexual reproduction

benchmark: Compare and contrast mitosis and meiosis and relate to the processes of sexual and asexual reproduction and their consequences for genetic variation.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to compare and contrast mitosis and meiosis, focusing on their roles in sexual and asexual reproduction and the implications for genetic variation. Items may include case studies or scenarios requiring deeper analysis.
essential_questions: What are the key differences between mitosis and meiosis?
How do these processes affect genetic variation in organisms?
In what contexts do mitosis and meiosis occur?
learning_targets: I can compare mitosis and meiosis.
I can explain the consequences of each process for genetic variation.
I can relate mitosis and meiosis to reproduction.
sample_assessment: Which statement accurately describes a difference between mitosis and meiosis? A) Mitosis produces haploid cells, while meiosis produces diploid cells.

benchmark: Explain the general distribution of life in aquatic systems as a function of chemistry, geography, light, depth, salinity, and temperature.
item_specs: Students must explain how abiotic factors determine the distribution of life in aquatic systems.
essential_questions: What abiotic factors affect life in aquatic ecosystems?
How does light availability change with depth?
learning_targets: I can explain how abiotic factors affect the distribution of aquatic organisms.
I can describe how light, temperature, and salinity influence aquatic life.
sample_assessment: Why is photosynthesis limited in the deep ocean? A) Too much oxygen B) Too many predators C) Light does not penetrate to great depths D) The water is too salty

benchmark: Describe changes in ecosystems resulting from seasonal variations, climate change and succession.
item_specs: Students must describe how ecosystems change over time.
essential_questions: How do ecosystems change through succession?
How does climate change affect ecosystems?
learning_targets: I can describe primary and secondary succession.
I can explain how seasonal variations and climate change affect ecosystems.
sample_assessment: Which is an example of primary succession? A) A forest regrowing after a fire B) Lichens colonizing bare rock C) Grass growing in an abandoned field D) Trees growing after logging

benchmark: Analyze how population size is determined by births, deaths, immigration, emigration, and limiting factors (biotic and abiotic) that determine carrying capacity.
item_specs: Students must analyze factors that determine population size and carrying capacity.
essential_questions: What factors determine population size?
What is carrying capacity?
learning_targets: I can analyze how births, deaths, immigration, and emigration affect population size.
I can explain how limiting factors determine carrying capacity.
sample_assessment: Which is a density-dependent limiting factor? A) Wildfire B) Flood C) Competition for food D) Volcanic eruption

benchmark: Compare and contrast the relationships among organisms, including predation, parasitism, competition, commensalism, and mutualism.
item_specs: Students must compare different types of ecological relationships.
essential_questions: What types of relationships exist between organisms?
How do symbiotic relationships differ?
learning_targets: I can compare predation, parasitism, competition, commensalism, and mutualism.
I can provide examples of each type of ecological relationship.
sample_assessment: A remora fish attaches to a shark for transportation and food scraps while the shark is unaffected. This is an example of: A) Mutualism B) Parasitism C) Commensalism D) Competition

benchmark: Recognize the consequences of the losses of biodiversity due to catastrophic events, climate changes, human activity, and the introduction of invasive, non-native species.
item_specs: Students must recognize consequences of biodiversity loss from various causes.
essential_questions: What causes biodiversity loss?
What are the consequences of introducing invasive species?
learning_targets: I can describe causes of biodiversity loss.
I can explain the impact of invasive species on ecosystems.
sample_assessment: The introduction of Burmese pythons in the Florida Everglades has resulted in: A) Increased biodiversity B) Decreased populations of native species C) No ecological impact D) More food for native predators

benchmark: Use a food web to identify and distinguish producers, consumers, and decomposers. Explain the pathway of energy transfer through trophic levels and the reduction of available energy at successive trophic levels. Interpret a Serengeti food web (B-O.1) Interpret an Antarctic food web (B-O.2)
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to analyze food webs to identify producers, consumers, and decomposers, as well as explain energy transfer through trophic levels. Items may include diagrams or scenarios that illustrate energy flow.
essential_questions: What roles do producers, consumers, and decomposers play in a food web?
How is energy transferred through different trophic levels?
What happens to energy as it moves up the food chain?
learning_targets: I can identify producers, consumers, and decomposers in a food web.
I can explain energy transfer through trophic levels.
I can analyze how energy availability changes at each trophic level.
sample_assessment: In a food web, which of the following is a producer? A) A lion B) A tree C) A mushroom D) A rabbit

benchmark: Evaluate the costs and benefits of renewable and nonrenewable resources, such as water, energy, fossil fuels, wildlife, and forests.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to analyze various scenarios involving renewable and nonrenewable resources, weighing their costs and benefits. Items may include case studies, data interpretation, and require justification of choices based on evidence.
essential_questions: What are the long-term impacts of relying on nonrenewable resources?
How can we balance the use of renewable and nonrenewable resources for sustainability?
What factors should be considered when evaluating the costs and benefits of resource use?
learning_targets: I can evaluate the benefits of renewable resources compared to nonrenewable resources.
I can analyze the costs associated with different energy sources.
I can justify my choices regarding resource use based on evidence.
sample_assessment: Which of the following is a benefit of using solar energy over fossil fuels? A) Higher emissions of greenhouse gases B) Depletion of natural resources C) Reduced air pollution D) Increased reliance on foreign oil

benchmark: Discuss the need for adequate monitoring of environmental parameters when making policy decisions.
item_specs: Assessment items should focus on the importance of monitoring environmental parameters and how this data influences policy decisions. Items may include interpreting graphs, evaluating case studies, and discussing the implications of data on policy.
essential_questions: Why is monitoring environmental parameters critical for effective policy-making?
How can data influence decisions regarding environmental protection?
What are the consequences of inadequate environmental monitoring?
learning_targets: I can explain the importance of monitoring environmental parameters.
I can analyze data to support policy decisions.
I can discuss the potential impacts of poor monitoring on the environment.
sample_assessment: What is one reason why environmental monitoring is essential for policy decisions? A) It reduces the need for regulations B) It provides data to assess environmental health C) It eliminates the need for public input D) It simplifies decision-making processes

benchmark: Predict the impact of individuals on environmental systems and examine how human lifestyles affect sustainability. The carbon cycle: geosphere processes and interactions (B-O.)
item_specs: Assessment items should challenge students to predict and evaluate the effects of human activities on environmental systems. Items may include scenario-based questions, modeling exercises, and require students to propose solutions for sustainability.
essential_questions: How do individual actions contribute to environmental change?
In what ways can we alter our lifestyles to promote sustainability?
What role does the carbon cycle play in understanding human impact on the environment?
learning_targets: I can predict how my actions affect the environment.
I can evaluate the sustainability of different lifestyles.
I can explain the processes of the carbon cycle and their relevance to human activity.
sample_assessment: Which of the following actions is likely to have the most positive impact on sustainability? A) Increasing fossil fuel consumption B) Reducing waste and recycling C) Expanding urban development D) Overfishing in local waters

benchmark: Describe the basic molecular structures and primary functions of the four major categories of biological macromolecules.
item_specs: Students must describe the structure and function of carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids.
essential_questions: What are the four major macromolecules?
How does the structure of each macromolecule relate to its function?
learning_targets: I can identify the four major categories of macromolecules.
I can describe the structure and function of each macromolecule.
sample_assessment: Which macromolecule is composed of amino acid monomers? A) Carbohydrate B) Lipid C) Protein D) Nucleic acid

benchmark: Identify the reactants, products, and basic functions of photosynthesis.
item_specs: Students must identify the reactants and products of photosynthesis.
essential_questions: What are the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis?
Where does photosynthesis occur?
learning_targets: I can identify the reactants and products of photosynthesis.
I can describe the overall function of photosynthesis.
sample_assessment: What are the products of photosynthesis? A) Carbon dioxide and water B) Glucose and oxygen C) Oxygen and carbon dioxide D) Water and ATP

benchmark: Identify the reactants, products, and basic functions of aerobic and anaerobic cellular respiration.
item_specs: Students must identify the reactants and products of cellular respiration.
essential_questions: What are the reactants and products of cellular respiration?
How do aerobic and anaerobic respiration differ?
learning_targets: I can identify the reactants and products of aerobic cellular respiration.
I can compare aerobic and anaerobic respiration.
sample_assessment: During aerobic respiration, glucose is broken down in the presence of: A) Carbon dioxide B) Nitrogen C) Oxygen D) Hydrogen

benchmark: Explain the interrelated nature of photosynthesis and cellular respiration.
item_specs: Students must explain how photosynthesis and cellular respiration are connected.
essential_questions: How are photosynthesis and cellular respiration related?
Why are both processes necessary for life?
learning_targets: I can explain how the products of photosynthesis are the reactants of cellular respiration.
I can describe the cycling of matter and energy between these processes.
sample_assessment: The products of photosynthesis are used as the reactants of: A) DNA replication B) Cellular respiration C) Protein synthesis D) Fermentation only

benchmark: Connect the role of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) to energy transfers within a cell.
item_specs: Students must explain the role of ATP as the energy currency of the cell.
essential_questions: What is the role of ATP in cells?
How does ATP store and release energy?
learning_targets: I can explain how ATP stores and releases energy.
I can describe how ATP is used in cellular processes.
sample_assessment: Energy is released from ATP when: A) A phosphate group is added B) A phosphate group is removed C) ATP is synthesized D) Glucose is broken down

benchmark: Explain the role of enzymes as catalysts that lower the activation energy of biochemical reactions. Identify factors, such as pH and temperature, and their effect on enzyme activity.
item_specs: Students must explain how enzymes function and what factors affect enzyme activity.
essential_questions: How do enzymes speed up chemical reactions?
What factors affect enzyme activity?
learning_targets: I can explain how enzymes lower activation energy.
I can describe how pH and temperature affect enzyme activity.
sample_assessment: If an enzyme is exposed to extremely high temperatures, it will: A) Work faster B) Denature and lose function C) Produce more substrate D) Lower the pH

benchmark: Discuss the special properties of water that contribute to Earth's suitability as an environment for life: cohesive behavior, ability to moderate temperature, expansion upon freezing, and versatility as a solvent.
item_specs: Students must describe the unique properties of water and their importance for life.
essential_questions: What makes water essential for life?
How do water's properties support living organisms?
learning_targets: I can describe the properties of water that make it essential for life.
I can explain how hydrogen bonding gives water its unique properties.
sample_assessment: Why does ice float in liquid water? A) Ice is denser than water B) Water expands upon freezing, making ice less dense C) Ice molecules move faster D) Gravity pushes ice up

benchmark: Define a problem based on a specific body of knowledge, for example: biology, chemistry, physics, and earth/space science, and do the following: pose questions about the natural world, conduct systematic observations, examine books and other sources of information to see what is already known, review what is known in light of empirical evidence, plan investigations, use tools to gather, analyze, and interpret data, pose answers, explanations, and predictions, and communicate results.
item_specs: Students must define problems, plan and conduct investigations, and analyze data using the scientific method.
essential_questions: How do scientists design and conduct investigations?
What is the role of evidence in scientific inquiry?
learning_targets: I can design a controlled experiment with independent and dependent variables.
I can collect, analyze, and interpret data using appropriate tools.
I can communicate results and draw conclusions based on evidence.
sample_assessment: A student wants to test whether fertilizer affects plant growth. Which is the independent variable? A) Amount of water B) Type of fertilizer C) Height of the plant D) Type of soil

benchmark: Describe and explain what characterizes science and its methods.
item_specs: Students must describe the characteristics that distinguish science from other ways of knowing.
essential_questions: What makes science different from other disciplines?
What are the key characteristics of scientific inquiry?
learning_targets: I can describe the characteristics of science.
I can explain how scientific methods lead to reliable knowledge.
sample_assessment: Which characteristic best distinguishes science from other ways of knowing? A) It relies on authority B) It is based on empirical evidence C) It never changes D) It provides absolute proof

benchmark: Recognize that the strength or usefulness of a scientific claim is evaluated through scientific argumentation, which depends on critical and logical thinking, and the active consideration of alternative scientific explanations to that claim.
item_specs: Students must evaluate scientific claims using evidence and logical reasoning.
essential_questions: How do scientists evaluate the strength of a claim?
Why is it important to consider alternative explanations?
learning_targets: I can evaluate scientific claims based on evidence.
I can use logical reasoning to assess alternative explanations.
sample_assessment: A scientist publishes a claim that a new drug cures a disease. What should other scientists do first? A) Accept the claim B) Design experiments to test the claim C) Ignore the claim D) Change the textbook

benchmark: Identify sources of information and assess their reliability according to the strict standards of scientific investigation.
item_specs: Students must evaluate the reliability of scientific information sources.
essential_questions: How do you determine if a source of scientific information is reliable?
What makes a scientific source credible?
learning_targets: I can identify reliable sources of scientific information.
I can assess source credibility using scientific standards.
sample_assessment: Which source would be most reliable for scientific research? A) A personal blog B) A peer-reviewed journal article C) A social media post D) A television advertisement

benchmark: Describe and provide examples of how similar investigations conducted in many parts of the world result in the same outcome.
item_specs: Students must explain why scientific investigations are repeatable regardless of location.
essential_questions: Why do scientific experiments produce the same results regardless of where they are conducted?
What does reproducibility mean in science?
learning_targets: I can explain why scientific investigations are reproducible.
I can provide examples of investigations that yield consistent results worldwide.
sample_assessment: Why would an experiment measuring the boiling point of water at sea level produce the same result in Japan and Brazil? A) Scientists communicate B) Natural laws are universal C) They use the same equipment D) The water is the same brand

benchmark: Describe how scientific inferences are drawn from scientific observations and provide examples from the content being studied.
item_specs: Students must distinguish between observations and inferences and explain how inferences are drawn.
essential_questions: What is the difference between an observation and an inference?
How do scientists use observations to make inferences?
learning_targets: I can distinguish between observations and inferences.
I can explain how inferences are drawn from observations.
sample_assessment: A scientist observes fossils of marine organisms on a mountain top. Which is an inference? A) The fossils are on the mountain B) The fossils are of marine organisms C) The area was once covered by ocean D) The mountain is tall

benchmark: Recognize the role of creativity in constructing scientific questions, methods and explanations.
item_specs: Students must recognize that creativity plays an important role in scientific discovery.
essential_questions: How does creativity contribute to scientific discovery?
Why is imagination important in science?
learning_targets: I can explain how creativity is used in developing scientific questions and methods.
I can provide examples of creative approaches in science.
sample_assessment: How did Watson and Crick demonstrate creativity in science? A) They memorized all known facts B) They built a creative model of DNA using available evidence C) They repeated other scientists' experiments D) They ignored previous research

benchmark: Identify what is science, what clearly is not science, and what superficially resembles science (but fails to meet the criteria for science).
item_specs: Students must distinguish between science and pseudoscience.
essential_questions: What criteria must be met for something to be considered science?
How can you identify pseudoscience?
learning_targets: I can identify the criteria that define science.
I can distinguish between science and pseudoscience.
sample_assessment: Which of the following is an example of pseudoscience? A) Testing a hypothesis in a lab B) Claiming crystals cure disease without evidence C) Publishing results in a peer-reviewed journal D) Replicating an experiment

benchmark: Identify which questions can be answered through science and which questions are outside the boundaries of scientific investigation, such as questions addressed by other ways of knowing, such as art, philosophy, and religion.
item_specs: Students must distinguish between questions that science can and cannot answer.
essential_questions: What types of questions can science answer?
What questions fall outside the scope of science?
learning_targets: I can identify questions that can be answered through scientific investigation.
I can recognize questions that are outside the boundaries of science.
sample_assessment: Which question can be answered through science? A) Is it wrong to clone animals? B) What is the meaning of life? C) Does temperature affect enzyme activity? D) Which painting is the most beautiful?

benchmark: Explain that scientific knowledge is both durable and robust and open to change. Scientific knowledge can change when new evidence or new interpretations of existing evidence are presented.
item_specs: Students must explain how scientific knowledge is durable yet subject to change with new evidence.
essential_questions: Why is scientific knowledge considered durable?
Under what conditions does scientific knowledge change?
learning_targets: I can explain why scientific knowledge is both durable and open to change.
I can provide examples of how new evidence has changed scientific understanding.
sample_assessment: The discovery of DNA structure changed biology. This is an example of: A) Science being unreliable B) Scientific knowledge being open to change with new evidence C) Scientists making mistakes D) Science being opinion-based

benchmark: Explain that a scientific theory is the culmination of many scientific investigations drawing together all the current evidence concerning a substantial range of phenomena; thus, a scientific theory represents the most powerful explanation scientists have to offer.
item_specs: Students must explain what constitutes a scientific theory and its role in science.
essential_questions: What makes a scientific theory powerful?
How is a scientific theory developed?
learning_targets: I can explain what constitutes a scientific theory.
I can describe how theories are supported by multiple lines of evidence.
sample_assessment: A scientific theory is best described as: A) An educated guess B) A comprehensive explanation supported by extensive evidence C) A fact that will never change D) A single experiment's conclusion

benchmark: Recognize that theories do not become laws, nor do laws become theories; theories are well supported explanations and laws are well supported descriptions.
item_specs: Students must distinguish between scientific theories and scientific laws.
essential_questions: What is the difference between a scientific theory and a scientific law?
Can a theory become a law?
learning_targets: I can distinguish between scientific theories and scientific laws.
I can explain why theories do not become laws.
sample_assessment: What is the relationship between scientific theories and laws? A) Theories become laws when proven B) Laws become theories over time C) They serve different functions: theories explain, laws describe D) They are the same thing

benchmark: Describe the function of models in science, and identify the wide range of models used in science.
item_specs: Students must describe how models are used in science and identify different types of models.
essential_questions: Why do scientists use models?
What are the limitations of scientific models?
learning_targets: I can describe the function of models in science.
I can identify different types of scientific models and their limitations.
sample_assessment: Why do scientists use models? A) Models are always exact replicas B) Models help explain and predict phenomena C) Models replace the need for experiments D) Models are never changed

benchmark: Explain how scientific knowledge and reasoning provide an empirically-based perspective to inform society's decision making.
item_specs: Students must explain how scientific knowledge informs societal decisions.
essential_questions: How does science inform public policy?
Why should society use scientific evidence in decision making?
learning_targets: I can explain how scientific knowledge informs societal decisions.
I can provide examples of science-informed policy decisions.
sample_assessment: How does scientific knowledge contribute to society's decision making about climate change? A) Scientists make the laws B) Scientific data provides evidence to inform policy decisions C) Science replaces government D) Science tells people what to believe

benchmark: Weigh the merits of alternative strategies for solving a specific societal problem by comparing a number of different costs and benefits, such as human, economic, and environmental.
item_specs: Students must compare strategies for solving societal problems by analyzing costs and benefits.
essential_questions: How do scientists and society weigh costs and benefits of different solutions?
What factors should be considered when choosing a strategy to solve a problem?
learning_targets: I can compare alternative strategies for solving societal problems.
I can analyze the human, economic, and environmental costs and benefits of different approaches.
sample_assessment: A community must decide between building a coal plant or a solar farm. Which factors should be weighed? A) Only cost B) Only environmental impact C) Human, economic, and environmental costs and benefits D) Only the speed of construction

benchmark: Differentiate among the four states of matter.
item_specs: Students must differentiate between solid, liquid, gas, and plasma.
essential_questions: What are the four states of matter?
How do the states of matter differ in particle arrangement and energy?
learning_targets: I can describe the four states of matter.
I can compare particle arrangement and energy in each state.
sample_assessment: Which state of matter has particles that are ionized and found in stars? A) Solid B) Liquid C) Gas D) Plasma

benchmark: Differentiate between physical and chemical properties and physical and chemical changes of matter.
item_specs: Students must differentiate between physical and chemical properties and changes.
essential_questions: What is the difference between physical and chemical properties?
How do you distinguish between physical and chemical changes?
learning_targets: I can differentiate between physical and chemical properties.
I can classify changes as physical or chemical.
sample_assessment: Which is a chemical change? A) Melting ice B) Cutting paper C) Rusting iron D) Dissolving sugar in water

benchmark: Explore the scientific theory of atoms (also known as atomic theory) by describing changes in the atomic model over time and why those changes were necessitated by experimental evidence.
item_specs: Students must describe how atomic models changed over time based on evidence.
essential_questions: How has the atomic model changed over time?
What evidence led to changes in the atomic model?
learning_targets: I can describe the evolution of atomic models.
I can explain how experimental evidence led to changes in atomic theory.
sample_assessment: Rutherford's gold foil experiment led to the discovery of: A) Electrons B) The nucleus C) Neutrons D) Protons orbiting electrons

benchmark: Explore the scientific theory of atoms (also known as atomic theory) by describing the structure of atoms in terms of protons, neutrons and electrons, and differentiate among these particles in terms of their mass, electrical charges and locations within the atom.
item_specs: Students must describe atomic structure and differentiate among subatomic particles.
essential_questions: What are the subatomic particles?
How do protons, neutrons, and electrons differ?
learning_targets: I can describe the structure of an atom.
I can differentiate among protons, neutrons, and electrons by mass, charge, and location.
sample_assessment: Which subatomic particle has a negative charge and is located outside the nucleus? A) Proton B) Neutron C) Electron D) Quark

benchmark: Relate properties of atoms and their position in the periodic table to the arrangement of their electrons.
item_specs: Students must relate atomic properties and periodic table position to electron arrangement.
essential_questions: How does an atom's electron configuration relate to its position in the periodic table?
What periodic trends are related to electron arrangement?
learning_targets: I can relate an element's position in the periodic table to its electron configuration.
I can describe periodic trends such as electronegativity and atomic radius.
sample_assessment: Elements in the same group of the periodic table have the same number of: A) Protons B) Neutrons C) Valence electrons D) Total electrons

benchmark: Distinguish between bonding forces holding compounds together and other attractive forces, including hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces.
item_specs: Students must distinguish between intramolecular bonds and intermolecular forces.
essential_questions: What is the difference between bonding forces and intermolecular forces?
How do hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces affect matter?
learning_targets: I can distinguish between ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds.
I can compare hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces.
sample_assessment: Which type of intermolecular force is responsible for the high boiling point of water? A) London dispersion forces B) Ionic bonding C) Hydrogen bonding D) Metallic bonding

benchmark: Interpret formula representations of molecules and compounds in terms of composition and structure and can convert between the name and formula of common compounds.
item_specs: Students must interpret chemical formulas and convert between names and formulas.
essential_questions: How do chemical formulas represent compounds?
How do you name chemical compounds?
learning_targets: I can interpret chemical formulas in terms of composition.
I can convert between names and formulas of common compounds.
sample_assessment: What is the chemical formula for calcium chloride? A) CaCl B) CaCl2 C) Ca2Cl D) Ca2Cl2

benchmark: Characterize types of chemical reactions, for example: synthesis, decomposition, single-replacement, and double-replacement reactions.
item_specs: Students must identify and characterize different types of chemical reactions.
essential_questions: What are the main types of chemical reactions?
How can you classify a chemical reaction?
learning_targets: I can identify synthesis, decomposition, single-replacement, and double-replacement reactions.
I can balance chemical equations.
sample_assessment: In the reaction 2H2 + O2 -> 2H2O, this is an example of a: A) Decomposition reaction B) Synthesis reaction C) Single replacement reaction D) Double replacement reaction

benchmark: Apply the mole concept and the law of conservation of mass to calculate quantities of chemicals participating in reactions.
item_specs: Students must apply the mole concept and conservation of mass in chemical calculations.
essential_questions: What is a mole in chemistry?
How does conservation of mass apply to chemical reactions?
learning_targets: I can use the mole concept to calculate quantities in chemical reactions.
I can apply the law of conservation of mass to balance equations and calculate products.
sample_assessment: How many moles of water are produced when 2 moles of hydrogen gas react with excess oxygen? A) 1 mole B) 2 moles C) 3 moles D) 4 moles

benchmark: Describe oxidation-reduction reactions in living and non-living systems.
item_specs: Students must describe oxidation-reduction reactions and identify oxidizing and reducing agents.
essential_questions: What happens during an oxidation-reduction reaction?
How are redox reactions important in living systems?
learning_targets: I can describe oxidation and reduction in terms of electron transfer.
I can identify redox reactions in biological and non-biological systems.
sample_assessment: In a redox reaction, the substance that loses electrons is: A) Reduced B) Oxidized C) Neutral D) A catalyst

benchmark: Relate acidity and basicity to hydronium and hydroxyl ion concentration and pH.
item_specs: Students must relate pH to ion concentrations and classify solutions as acidic or basic.
essential_questions: What determines whether a solution is acidic or basic?
How is pH related to hydronium ion concentration?
learning_targets: I can relate pH to hydronium and hydroxide ion concentrations.
I can classify solutions as acidic, basic, or neutral using the pH scale.
sample_assessment: A solution with a pH of 3 is: A) Neutral B) Strongly basic C) Strongly acidic D) Weakly basic

benchmark: Describe the properties of the carbon atom that make the diversity of carbon compounds possible.
item_specs: Students must describe why carbon can form such a diverse array of compounds.
essential_questions: Why is carbon able to form so many different compounds?
What properties of carbon enable organic chemistry?
learning_targets: I can describe the bonding properties of carbon.
I can explain why carbon forms the basis of organic chemistry.
sample_assessment: Carbon can form four covalent bonds because it has: A) 4 protons B) 4 valence electrons C) 4 neutrons D) 4 energy levels

benchmark: Identify selected functional groups and relate how they contribute to properties of carbon compounds.
item_specs: Students must identify functional groups and relate them to compound properties.
essential_questions: What are functional groups?
How do functional groups affect the properties of organic compounds?
learning_targets: I can identify common functional groups.
I can explain how functional groups affect the properties of organic compounds.
sample_assessment: Which functional group is characteristic of alcohols? A) Carboxyl (-COOH) B) Amino (-NH2) C) Hydroxyl (-OH) D) Phosphate (-PO4)

benchmark: Differentiate among the various forms of energy and recognize that they can be transformed from one form to others.
item_specs: Students must identify forms of energy and describe energy transformations.
essential_questions: What are the different forms of energy?
How is energy transformed from one form to another?
learning_targets: I can identify different forms of energy.
I can describe how energy is transformed from one form to another.
sample_assessment: When a battery powers a flashlight, the energy transformation is: A) Light to chemical B) Chemical to electrical to light C) Nuclear to light D) Mechanical to thermal

benchmark: Explore the Law of Conservation of Energy by differentiating among open, closed, and isolated systems and explaining that the total energy in an isolated system is a conserved quantity.
item_specs: Students must explain the law of conservation of energy in different types of systems.
essential_questions: What is the Law of Conservation of Energy?
How do open, closed, and isolated systems differ?
learning_targets: I can explain the Law of Conservation of Energy.
I can differentiate among open, closed, and isolated systems.
sample_assessment: In an isolated system, the total energy: A) Increases over time B) Decreases over time C) Remains constant D) Fluctuates randomly
